era_context: The late 14th century was characterized by major geopolitical upheaval: the Ottoman Empire was expanding aggressively in the Balkans, the Timurid Empire dominated Central Asia and the Middle East, the Ming Dynasty was consolidating Han Chinese rule after the Yuan collapse, and Western European kingdoms were engaged in dynastic conflicts. The Western Schism in the Catholic Church (1378–1417) was creating religious and political instability. This was an era of significant military innovation, early maritime exploration, and the emergence of early modern state structures.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in Old World sources)

events.title: Establishment of the Joseon Dynasty in Korea
events.description: The Joseon Dynasty was established in Korea in 1392, following the collapse of the Goryeo Dynasty. General Yi Seong-gye founded the new dynasty, which would rule Korea for over 500 years until 1910. The establishment of Joseon marked a significant shift in Korean politics and culture, with the new dynasty adopting Neo-Confucianism as its state ideology and establishing a more centralized administrative system. The founding of Joseon had important implications for East Asian geopolitics, as it created a new major power in the region and shifted the balance of power in relation to the Ming Dynasty in China.
key_figures: Yi Seong-gye (Taejo, founder of Joseon Dynasty, r. 1392–1398)

sources.name: Joseon Dynasty official records (Joseon Wangjo Sillok)
Chinese Ming Dynasty records documenting Korean relations

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Joseon Dynasty in 1392 is well-documented in both Korean and Chinese sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader East Asian geopolitical shifts in the late 14th century

events.title: Ottoman consolidation in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: Bayezid I continued consolidating Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans in 1392, eliminating rival Turkish beyliks and extending Ottoman control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. This period saw the Ottoman Empire transition from a regional power to a major Mediterranean and Balkan force. The consolidation involved both military campaigns and diplomatic maneuvering, setting the stage for the major Ottoman expansion of the late 1390s.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation in this period is well-attested in general, but specific events of 1392 are less precisely documented than some other years.
cross_references: Part of Ottoman expansion documented from 1389 onward

events.title: Ming Dynasty's consolidation of Han Chinese rule
events.description: The Ming Dynasty, established in 1368, continued consolidating Han Chinese rule over China in 1392. The Hongwu Emperor was in the later years of his reign, and the period saw the establishment of Ming administrative structures, the suppression of remaining Yuan loyalists, and the beginning of major construction projects. The consolidation laid the groundwork for the Ming Dynasty's later maritime expansion and cultural flowering.
events.certainty_note: Ming consolidation in the 1390s is well-attested in general. Specific events of 1392 are less precisely documented but fit within the broader pattern of administrative consolidation.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty consolidation documented from 1368 onward

events.title: Timur's consolidation of Central Asian power
events.description: Timur continued consolidating his vast empire in 1392, which stretched from the Mediterranean to China. He was organizing administrative structures and preparing for further campaigns. His court at Samarkand was becoming a major center of Islamic culture, architecture, and learning. Timur's dominance shaped the geopolitical landscape of Central Asia, the Middle East, and parts of South Asia.
key_figures: Timur (Tamerlane, Timurid Empire founder and ruler)

sources.name: Persian chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi's Zafarnama)

events.certainty_note: Timur's political dominance in 1392 is well-attested. Specific events of this year are less precisely documented than some other years.
cross_references: Part of Timurid expansion documented from 1370s onward

disconfirming_evidence: The founding of the Joseon Dynasty is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward 'revolution' against the Goryeo Dynasty, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was a gradual transition with significant continuity in administrative structures and personnel. Additionally, Yi Seong-gye's motivations were complex, involving both genuine reform impulses and personal political ambition. The characterization of the founding as purely ideological (Neo-Confucianism vs. Buddhism) obscures the political and military factors that were equally important.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1392 is moderate, with relatively strong coverage of East Asian, Ottoman, and Ming political history. Korean sources on the Joseon founding are abundant and detailed. Ottoman and Chinese sources are also available, though they reflect court biases. Persian and Central Asian sources on Timur are available but often reflect the perspective of the conqueror. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific histories remain largely absent from surviving written records for this year. The founding of Joseon is one of the most significant events of 1392 and represents an important shift in East Asian geopolitics that is sometimes underrepresented in Western-centric historical narratives.

graph_edges.note: The founding of Joseon in 1392 occurred during the same period as the Yongle Emperor's rise to power in Ming China in 1402, both representing major East Asian geopolitical shifts
Ottoman consolidation in 1392 set the stage for the Battle of Nicopolis in 1396
Ming consolidation in 1392 set the stage for the succession crisis after the Hongwu Emperor's death in 1398
